hero.title: Kubernetes Interview Questions 2026
hero.description: Essential Kubernetes interview preparation for DevOps engineers and platform engineers. Covers K8s architecture, workloads, networking, security, and Helm.

questions.q: Explain the Kubernetes architecture (control plane vs worker nodes).
questions.a: Control Plane: API Server (central hub for all communication), etcd (distributed key-value store, cluster state), Scheduler (assigns Pods to nodes), Controller Manager (maintains desired state via controllers). Worker Nodes: kubelet (runs and monitors Pods), kube-proxy (network rules), Container Runtime (Docker/containerd). All communication goes through the API Server.

questions.q: What is etcd and what happens if it goes down?
questions.a: etcd is Kubernetes' backing store — a distributed key-value database that stores all cluster state. If etcd goes down, the control plane stops functioning: no new scheduling, updates, or deployments. Existing Pods continue running (kubelet maintains them locally). Always run etcd with 3 or 5 replicas for HA and take regular snapshots for disaster recovery.

questions.q: What is the Kubernetes Scheduler and how does it work?
questions.a: The Scheduler watches for unscheduled Pods and assigns them to appropriate nodes. Filtering phase: eliminates nodes that don't meet requirements (resources, node selectors, taints). Scoring phase: ranks remaining nodes by priority (resource availability, spread). Assigns Pod to highest-scoring node. Node Affinity, Taints/Tolerations, and Pod Affinity influence scheduling decisions.

questions.q: What is a Kubernetes PersistentVolume (PV) and PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)?
questions.a: PV: a piece of storage provisioned in the cluster (static or dynamic). PVC: a user's request for storage (specifying size, access modes). Kubernetes binds a PVC to a matching PV. StorageClasses enable dynamic provisioning — when a PVC is created, a PV is automatically created in the cloud (e.g., AWS EBS volume). StatefulSets use PVCs to persist data across Pod restarts.

questions.q: What is a DaemonSet and when do you use it?
questions.a: A DaemonSet ensures one Pod runs on every (or selected) node. Automatically adds Pods to new nodes. Used for: log collectors (Fluentd), monitoring agents (Prometheus Node Exporter), network plugins (CNI), security agents. Cannot scale horizontally like Deployments — exactly one per node.

questions.q: What are Kubernetes resource requests and limits?
questions.a: Requests: minimum resources a container needs (used for scheduling decisions). Limits: maximum resources a container can use. If a container exceeds CPU limit, it's throttled. If it exceeds memory limit, it's OOMKilled (Out of Memory). Best practice: always set both. QoS classes: Guaranteed (requests==limits), Burstable (limits > requests), BestEffort (no requests/limits).

Every Pod gets a unique IP. All Pods can communicate with all other Pods without NAT (flat network). Services provide stable virtual IPs. kube-proxy implements service load balancing using iptables or IPVS rules. CNI plugins (Calico, Flannel, Cilium) implement the actual network overlay. Ingress controllers handle external HTTP traffic routing.

Role-Based Access Control restricts who can perform what actions on which resources. Components: Role (defines permissions within a namespace), ClusterRole (cluster-wide permissions), RoleBinding (grants Role to a user/serviceAccount in a namespace), ClusterRoleBinding. Always follow least privilege. Use ServiceAccounts for Pod identities, not user accounts.

questions.q: What is a NetworkPolicy in Kubernetes?
questions.a: NetworkPolicies are firewall rules for Pods, controlling which Pods can communicate with which. By default, all Pods can communicate freely. A NetworkPolicy with podSelector {} and no ingress/egress rules blocks all traffic to selected Pods. Requires a CNI plugin that supports NetworkPolicy (Calico, Cilium — NOT Flannel).

topTips: The CKA exam is practical — practice kubectl commands until they're muscle memory.
Understand Pod lifecycle states: Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, Unknown, CrashLoopBackOff.
Know how to debug: kubectl describe pod, kubectl logs, kubectl exec -it, kubectl get events.
Understand Service types: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (expose on node), LoadBalancer (cloud LB), ExternalName (DNS alias).
Practice HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) configuration — scaling based on CPU/custom metrics is commonly asked.
